ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general paratha entry; no specific mint/pudina paratha code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific paratha or pudina paratha code. Unleavened bread category A00LC used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for pudina paratha or paratha flatbread. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium pudina paratha weighing approximately 90 g, as typically served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Paratha, whole wheat, plain — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole wheat flour and ghee for micronutrient estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for plain whole wheat paratha cooked with fat. Pudina paratha is a mint-stuffed variant; mint leaves contribute negligible macronutrients at typical inclusion levels (~5–8 g per 100 g finished product). Values adapted from plain whole wheat paratha with ghee topping. Micronutrients for potassium, calcium, iron, and B-vitamins cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20080, whole wheat flour) and USDA entry for ghee (NDB 04134). Energy calculated using Atwater factors where direct values were unavailable.

gi_source.title: Chapati / whole wheat flatbread GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole wheat chapati/roti
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pudina paratha specifically. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: whole wheat chapati/roti (GI ~62, medium category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. Pudina paratha uses the same whole wheat atta base; the addition of mint leaves does not materially alter glycemic response. The ghee topping slightly reduces GI relative to a dry chapati by slowing gastric emptying; however, the dominant determinant is the whole wheat flour matrix. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 41.4) / 100 ≈ 25.7, rounded to 25.6 (serving carbohydrate: 46.0 g/100g × 90 g serving = 41.4 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) and ghee-derived saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pudina paratha to one piece per meal and avoid additional salt or high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys, as the combined sodium load can approach 280–300 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate glycemic load (~25.6 per 90 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume pudina paratha in controlled portions (one piece) and pair it with high-fibre vegetables or a protein-rich dal to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the whole wheat base provides dietary fibre (4.5 g/100 g) which moderates glycemic impact compared to refined flour alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~6.8 g/100 g) contributing to LDL cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit ghee-topped paratha to occasional consumption and consider requesting reduced ghee or substituting with a small amount of unsaturated oil, as the saturated fat content (6.8 g/100 g) can contribute meaningfully to daily saturated fat limits when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pudina Paratha is a traditional North Indian whole wheat flatbread (paratha) filled with freshly chopped mint leaves (pudina) and finished with a generous topping of hot ghee (clarified butter). The dough is made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and salt, rolled into thin layers with the mint filling enclosed within, then pan-cooked on a tawa (griddle) until golden-brown with characteristic charred spots. The result is a flaky, layered bread with a fragrant herbal aroma from the mint and a rich, buttery flavour from the ghee. Texture is soft on the inside with slightly crisp outer layers. Pudina paratha is commonly served as a breakfast or lunch item in North India and is popular in Indian restaurants across Singapore, typically accompanied by yoghurt (dahi), pickle (achar), or a lentil curry (dal). Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~326 kcal/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre from whole wheat (4.5 g/100 g), and plant-based iron (2.1 mg/100 g). The ghee topping contributes saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g), which is clinically relevant for cardiovascular and lipid management. The medium glycemic index (~62) means blood glucose response is moderate; portion control is advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The mint filling contributes negligible macronutrients but adds vitamin C (~2.5 mg/100 g) and antioxidant phytonutrients. This dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a mint paratha?
Is pudina paratha good for diabetics?
What is the GI of paratha?
Is paratha made with whole wheat?
What does pudina mean in Indian food?

ingredient_synonyms.paratha: layered flatbread, Indian flatbread, whole wheat bread

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain whole wheat paratha with ghee, which is the closest approved primary source. No direct ICMR-NIN entry for pudina paratha exists. Mint leaves contribute negligible macronutrients at typical inclusion levels. GI is an analogous estimate from whole wheat chapati/roti data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact pudina paratha entry was found in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) plain whole wheat paratha entry, adjusted for ghee topping. Mint leaves (~5–8 g per 100 g finished product) contribute negligible macronutrients and are not expected to materially alter the macronutrient profile. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pudina paratha is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients except ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA data for this food type. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category; ghee contains trace vitamin D but a reliable per-100g figure for this prepared dish is not available from approved sources. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for wheat-based flatbreads. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from whole wheat chapati/roti (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008). No direct GI measurement for pudina paratha was found in approved sources. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (62 × 41.4) / 100 ≈ 25.6, based on 90 g serving size and 46.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302397 is used as closest parent (general paratha); no specific pudina paratha FoodOn code exists. FoodEx2 code A00LC (unleavened bread) is used as closest parent. 8. Sodium estimate (310 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt and ghee; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g estimated from USDA SR Legacy ghee and whole wheat flour composition data.
